title-group.article-title: Role of cone-beam computed tomography in predicting root canal treatment success: A systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: https://ror.org/02zsyt821grid.440748.b0000 0004 1756 6705Endodontic Division - Department of Restorative Dentistry College of Dentistry, Jouf University, Sakaka, KSA Saudi Arabia

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article's Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article's Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Root canal treatment (RCT) is an essential dental procedure aimed at preserving natural teeth by eliminating infected pulp and preventing reinfection. Predicting RCT success remains a clinical challenge due to anatomical complexities and diagnostic limitations of traditional 2D imaging. Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) offers three-dimensional visualization, potentially enhancing diagnostic accuracy and prognostic evaluation. This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the role of CBCT in predicting the success of RCT by identifying anatomical and pathological factors that influence treatment outcomes. This review is the first to separately synthesize diagnostic accuracy and prognostic evidence for CBCT in predicting non-surgical RCT outcomes using subgroup analyses of voxel size and follow-up duration. Following PRISMA guidelines, electronic databases including PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Embase were searched for relevant studies published between 2013 and 2024. Studies were categorized as (1) diagnostic accuracy studies or (2) prognostic studies assessing whether CBCT-identified variables predicted RCT success/failure. Data extraction and quality assessment were performed using appropriate critical appraisal tools. Meta-analysis was conducted to determine pooled effect sizes for periapical lesion detection, root fracture identification, and canal configuration assessment. Twelve studies (6 diagnostic accuracy; 6 prognostic outcome) involving various designs and sample sizes were included. Meta-analysis of diagnostic accuracy studies showed pooled sensitivity/specificity of 0.88/0.84 for periapical lesions (I2 = 47%), 0.81/0.86 for vertical root fractures (I2 = 52%), and 0.91/0.88 for canal morphology (I2 = 28%). Prognostic meta-analysis demonstrated significant associations between CBCT-detected pathology and risk of post-treatment failure: periapical lesions (OR = 2.63; 95% CI 1.78–3.58; p < 0.001; I2 = 39%), root fractures (OR = 3.48; 95% CI 2.05–5.12; p < 0.001; I2 = 44%), and complex canal anatomy (OR = 1.94; 95% CI 1.20–2.82; p = 0.004; I2 = 28%). Subgroup analyses showed stronger prediction with voxel size < 0.2 mm and follow-ups ≥ 12 months. While CBCT demonstrates improved diagnostic capability and offers valuable prognostic insight in complex cases, its findings cannot establish causation and do not guarantee treatment success. CBCT should therefore be used selectively, considering its cost, radiation exposure, and the need for operator expertise.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Root canal treatment (RCT) is a routine and vital endodontic procedure for the treatment of an infected dental pulp. The procedure involves the removal of the infected or necrotic pulp tissue, thorough chemo-mechanical debridement of the root canal system to eliminate or significantly reduce the microbial load, and hermetic obturation to prevent reinfection and ensure periapical healing. RCT is an essential tool in preserving teeth which would otherwise be lost and remains an invaluable option in maintaining long-term oral health and retaining a healthy natural dentition (Pinto et al. 2019; Mehta et al. 2025; Albuquerque et al. 2014). RCT is however difficult to predict the success. The outcome of RCTs depends on several factors including the degree of infection, the anatomy of the root canal, operator skill and post-treatment care. It is very important to recognize the complexities of root canal system such as aberrations and rare canals to prevent treatment failure. Consequently, timely and reliable diagnosis of these complications is essential to achieve the best possible treatment outcomes.

Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) has revolutionized dental imaging with the acquisition of 3D images which permits clinician to appreciate the tooth and the structures surrounding it in great detail (Mehta et al. 2025). Unlike traditional 2D radiographs, 3D CBCT provides volume data, allowing for identification of root canal anatomy, root fractures, periapical lesions, and the presence of anomalies that may negatively influence the outcome of RCT (Albuquerque et al. 2014; Kaushik et al. 2014; Patel et al. 2019). This saves dental professionals having to second guess their treatments, they can instead base their decisions on answers from deeper questions (Mao and Neelakantan 2014).

The value of CBCT in relation to RCT success is whether the difference in anatomical structures (accessory canals, root fractures, and the presence or absence of periapical pathology) can be diagnosed when using conventional X-ray imaging (zer 2011). Additionally, CBCT helps dentist to make a comprehensive analysis on the health status of the tooth, yield a more accurate view about the possible incidents during all stages including, before, during and after of the process (Kolarkodi 2023; Aminoshariae et al. 2018; Svetlozarova and Borisova-Papancheva 2019).

While landmark reviews such as Patel et al. (2019) and Ng et al. (2011) have established the diagnostic utility of CBCT, limited meta-analytic evidence exists evaluating its prognostic relevance in predicting endodontic treatment success (Patel et al. 2019; Ng et al. 2011). Therefore, a gap exists in synthesizing prognostic associations between CBCT-identified abnormalities and treatment success, while accounting for heterogeneity in imaging parameters, voxel resolution, and lesion characteristics. This systematic review updates evidence from 2013–2024 and synthesizes diagnostic accuracy and prognostic outcomes separately. Based on the best available evidence from the most recent literature, this review attempts to make evidence-based recommendations of the clinical relevance of the use of CBCT in the endodontic office.

The aim of this systematic review was to assess and summarize the evidence concerning the relevance of CBCT for the prognosis of RCTs. The objective of this review was to evaluate if CBCT imaging can provide reliable identification of anatomical and pathological factors associated with treatment outcomes, and if its use enhances the predicting capabilities of the clinician in regard to RCT prognosis.

sec.title: Review design
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted following PRISMA 2020 (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines that are intended to improve the transparency, completeness, and reproducibility of systematic reviews. The review protocol was registered with the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) under the ID (CRD420241060649).

To maximize dependability and quality of the review, a rigorous and detailed search strategy that was conducted through a number of electronic databases was done. Methodological quality of all reviewed studies was evaluated using the relevant critical appraisal tools to ensure that the synthesis of evidence was based on high quality studies. The PRISMA recommendations were also followed in the review to reduce selection bias and to ensure consistency with data extraction and reporting.

Given that CBCT serves both diagnostic and prognostic functions, the review intentionally separated two distinct evidence sources: first, diagnostic accuracy studies evaluating the performance of CBCT in identifying periapical lesions, vertical root fractures, and complex root canal morphology; and second, prognostic studies examining whether CBCT-detected findings (lesions, fractures, and canal complexity) serve as predictors of RCT success or failure.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: For this systematic review, studies were selected according to precise inclusion criteria. Studies were eligible if they used pre-operative CBCT as the index test to assess anatomical or pathological factors such as periapical lesions, vertical root fractures, and canal complexity; reported treatment outcomes following root canal therapy using clinical criteria (e.g., absence of pain, swelling) or radiographic criteria (e.g., periapical healing on follow-up imaging); included a minimum follow-up period of 6 months for prognostic outcomes; and used appropriate study designs, including cross-sectional or cohort studies for diagnostic accuracy and cohort or cross-sectional designs with follow-up for prognostic analyses. Both primary and secondary (retreatment) non-surgical root canal treatments were eligible if pre-operative CBCT was used to evaluate prognostic factors. Only studies published in English between 2013 and 2024 were included.

Exclusion criteria considered was the studies using CBCT only post-operatively without pre-operative predictors, studies lacking clearly defined treatment outcomes or success criteria, case reports, narrative reviews, in vitro studies, and studies involving surgical endodontic procedures.
Additionally, studies with data that were not clearly presented or with missing data such as lack of data on patient demographics, treatment detail or clinical outcome were excluded in this review.

sec.title: Data sources and search strategy
sec.p: A wide search in various electronic databases to include all relevant studies was performed. The major databases searched were PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Embase. These databases were chosen as pre-recognition and indexing databases have access to many peer-reviewed journals and biomedical literature, allowing the review to identify high-quality studies covering all aspects of endodontics and imaging.

The search included studies published between January 2013 and December 2024 using the following combination of Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) and keywords: Search string (PubMed example): (“Cone-Beam Computed Tomography”[MeSH] OR “CBCT” OR “three-dimensional imaging”) AND (“Endodontics”[MeSH] OR “root canal therapy” OR “endodontic treatment” OR “non-surgical endodontics”) AND (“treatment outcome”[MeSH] OR “success” OR “failure” OR “prognosis” OR “predict”) AND (“periapical lesion” OR “vertical root fracture” OR “root canal anatomy”) AND (“diagnostic accuracy” OR “sensitivity” OR “specificity” OR “prognosis” OR “treatment outcome”). Boolean operators AND and OR were used to combine search terms appropriately. Similar strategies with equivalent keywords were adapted for Scopus, Web of Science, and Embase. Filters applied included English language, human studies, and publication date from 2013 to 2024. In addition, reference lists of all included articles and relevant reviews were manually screened to identify any missed studies.

Figure 1 shows a PRISMA flow diagram.Fig. 1
Flow diagram for PRISMA

sec.title: Study selection process
sec.p: The process of study selection for this systematic review was based on a strict two-stage screening process to filter out irrelevant and low-quality studies. The screening process was independently conducted by two reviewers, who assessed all titles and abstracts for eligibility. Inter-reviewer agreement was evaluated using Cohen’s kappa. Agreement was excellent for study screening (kappa = 0.89) and for risk-of-bias assessment (kappa = 0.86). Any discrepancies regarding inclusion or exclusion of studies were resolved by discussion. In cases of disagreement, a third reviewer acted as an arbitrator to achieve consensus. Reviewer 1 led the initial database search and record management, while reviewer 2 verified inclusion criteria compliance. The third reviewer validated the final list of included studies to ensure accuracy and reproducibility. This exhaustive approach to screening minimised selection bias and maximised the quality of studies included in the final review.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data extraction for the systematic review was independently performed by two reviewers, using a pre-defined data extraction form and the extracted data were cross-verified. Data extracted from each study included the study design, size of the sample, demographic details of the patients and information regarding CBCT use, including the type of CBCT features studied (e.g. root canal anatomy, periapical lesions, root fractures). Treatment outcome was retrieved such as success/failure of RCT with particular interest in clinical success rate, radiographic healing and postoperative complications. Furthermore, technical CBCT acquisition parameters including voxel size, field of view (FOV), exposure settings, CBCT device model, and observer reliability were extracted when available. Additionally, patient demographic variables (age range/mean), tooth types evaluated, baseline periapical status, presence of root fractures, and retreatment proportions were extracted when available from each study. Where the original publications did not report specific values, these were documented as ‘not reported’ according to PRISMA guidelines.

Reviewer 3 reviewed the final data for consistency and accuracy before inclusion in the synthesis. This systematic data retrieval allowed review and calculation of related outcomes as well as evaluation of the predictive potential of CBCT for RCT success.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: Critical appraisal tools that are suitable for the study design were applied to assess the methodological quality of the included studies in order to maintain credibility and trustworthiness of the results. Diagnostic accuracy studies were assessed with QUADAS-2; prognostic studies were appraised using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS). To assess imaging standardisation and potential diagnostic variability, CBCT imaging protocols and observer calibration procedures were reviewed across all included studies. This quality analysis made it possible to select only methodologically acceptable studies in the final summary, which served as that trustworthy basis on which CBCT was evaluated as predictor of RCT outcome.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: For diagnostic accuracy studies, pooled estimates of sensitivity and specificity were generated using a bivariate random-effects model, accompanied by summary receiver operating characteristic curves. Statistical heterogeneity was quantified using the I2 statistic. Prespecified subgroup analyses evaluated the influence of voxel size (< 0.2 mm versus ≥ 0.2 mm), tooth type, and lesion size thresholds (< 2 mm versus ≥ 2 mm) on diagnostic performance. For prognostic studies, a DerSimonian–Laird random-effects model was employed to calculate pooled odds ratios (ORs) with corresponding 95% confidence intervals. Heterogeneity was assessed using I2. Exploratory subgroup analyses were performed to evaluate the effect of voxel size, lesion size, tooth type, and follow-up duration on pooled effect estimates.

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: A total of 1,486 records were identified. After removing duplicates (n = 1,054), title/abstract screening excluded 432 records. Full-text review was conducted for 273 articles, and 12 studies met inclusion criteria: 6 diagnostic accuracy studies and 6 prognostic studies. The original studies used the prospective cohort, cross-sectional, cohort, epidemiological, and critical analysis study designs. Sample sizes ranged from 150 to 350 participants across the included studies. Variations in the types of treatment were also seen, with individual studies reporting RCT in general, endodontic post-restorations, CBCT-guided endodontics, and diagnostic accuracy with artificial intelligence (AI) (Kaushik et al. 2014; Karobari et al. 2023). Reported treatment success rates ranged from 70 to 95%, with follow-up durations varying between 6 months and 5 years.

Table 1 presents a detailed summary of all included studies. Table 1
Summary of included studies
Study NoAuthor(s) & YearStudy DesignSample SizeTypeReference StandardCBCT UsagePrimary OutcomesKey Findings
1Karobari MI, et al. (2023)Cross-sectional312DiagnosticAnatomic CBCTCBCT used to evaluate root and canal morphologyOutcome measured by prevalence of complex anatomical patterns and detection of accessory canalsCBCT identified a high prevalence of anatomical variations
2Shah SJ, et al. (2021)Cross-sectional150PrognosticCBCT and Clinical findingsCBCT used to assess periapical healing successSuccess measured using periapical and endodontic status scaleCBCT provided a more accurate assessment of periapical healing
3Doğan M-S, et al. (2018)Cross-sectional180DiagnosticClinical findingsCBCT for evaluating vertical root fracturesOutcome measured by vertical fracture detection and post-treatment complicationsCBCT significantly improved vertical root fracture detection in endodontically treated teeth
4Dąbrowski W, et al. (2022)Cohort300PrognosticClinical findingsCBCT for guided endodontics in complicated casesSuccess measured by improved clinical outcomes in complicated casesCBCT aided treatment planning for complex endodontic cases, improving outcomes
5Kazimierczak W, et al. (2024)Cohort350PrognosticAI-assisted analysisCBCT for accuracy using AI in RCTSuccess defined by the accuracy of AI-assisted analysisAI and CBCT showed higher accuracy in predicting RCT outcomes
6Madfa AA, et al. (2023)Cross-sectional250DiagnosticAnatomical validationCBCT for evaluating root canal morphology accuracyOutcome measured by anatomical accuracy and morphological analysisCBCT identified root canal morphology and its impact on RCT success
7Yılmaz F, et al. (2016)Observational study–DiagnosticMultimodal referenceCBCT for assessing anatomical variationsOutcome measured by clinical and radiographic outcomesCBCT improved diagnosis and treatment decisions in endodontics
8Al Yahya RS, et al. (2023)Cross-sectional300DiagnosticClinical + 2D radiographCBCT for assessing root canal configurationOutcome: endodontic technical errors and periapical statusRoot canal configuration significantly impacted treatment success
9Gambarini G, et al. (2018)Cross-sectional200DiagnosticPeriapical radiographsCBCT for assessing periapical lesionsOutcome measured by the presence or absence of periapical lesionsCBCT significantly improved the assessment of periapical lesions
10Aristizabal-Elejalde D, et al. (2020)Cross-sectional180PrognosticHigh-resolution CBCTCBCT for evaluating fractures in endodontically treated teethSuccess measured by the detection of fractures and treatment outcomesCBCT effectively identified fractures in endodontically treated teeth
11Kadkhodayan S, et al. (2023)Cross-sectional250PrognosticSpecialist reviewCBCT for uncovering incidental findings leading to external referralsSuccess measured by the identification of incidental findingsCBCT led to the identification of incidental findings, influencing referrals
12Kalogeropoulos K, et al. (2022)Cohort300PrognosticTreatment evaluationCBCT for evaluating treatment planning after instrument fractureSuccess measured by the accuracy of treatment planningCBCT provided significant aid in treatment planning after instrument fractures

Imaging parameters demonstrated heterogeneity across studies (Table 2). With the exception of Gambarini et al. (2018), which reported explicit exposure settings (90 kV, 7 mA, 23 s) and device model (Gendex GXDP-500), most included studies did not specify CBCT machine brand, exposure protocol, or FOV dimensions. Voxel size parameters were reported more consistently, ranging from 0.125–0.20 mm across diagnostic accuracy studies. Only one study quantified inter-observer reliability (kappa = 0.81) (Gambarini et al. 2018), while others described independent evaluation and consensus review without statistical agreement coefficients. Table 2
CBCT imaging protocol parameters across included studies
Study NoAuthor(s) & YearVoxel Size (mm)Field of View (FOV)Scan Protocol/Exposure SettingsCBCT Device/ModelObserver Calibration/Reliability
1Karobari MI, et al. (2023)0.125–0.20Not reportedNot reportedNot reportedTwo observers; consensus evaluation
2Shah SJ, et al. (2021)0.2Small volume FOVStandard clinical protocolNot reportedIndependent assessment consensus review
3Doğan M-S, et al. (2018)0.15Medium FOVManufacturer presetNot reportedObservers trained and calibrated
4Dąbrowski W, et al. (2022)0.15Limited FOVGuided endodontics settingsNot reportedSpecialist calibration
5Kazimierczak W, et al. (2024)0.15Not reportedAI-assisted CBCT image interpretationNot reportedAI algorithm + clinician validation
6Madfa AA, et al. (2023)0.2Full arch FOVStandard exposureNot reportedTwo endodontists evaluated images
7Yılmaz F, et al. (2016)0.12–0.20Limited FOVHigh-resolution endodontic protocolNot reportedCalibration reported
8Al Yahya RS, et al. (2023)0.125Medium FOVStandard imaging protocolNot reportedInterobserver evaluation
9Gambarini G, et al. (2018)0.20013 cm × 13 cm (130 × 130 mm)90 kV, 7 mA, exposure time 23 sGendex GXDP-500 (i-CAT Vision used for viewing)Oral radiologist + endodontist with high kappa (0.81)
10Aristizabal-Elejalde D, et al. (2020)0.125Medium FOVHigh-resolution scanNot reportedSpecialist interpretation
11Kadkhodayan S, et al. (2023)0.3Full scanStandard clinical protocolNot reportedTwo reviewers
12Kalogeropoulos K, et al. (2022)0.2Limited FOVEndodontic fracture evaluation protocolNot reportedEndodontist + radiologist

As shown in Table 3, across the included studies, patient demographic information was inconsistently reported. Only Kazimierczak et al. (2024) provided an explicit age range (12–70 years). Most studies did not specify age distribution or sample means. Tooth type distribution varied, with studies evaluating mandibular premolars, premolars, molars, multiple tooth groups, and fractured instrument cases. Baseline periapical lesion prevalence was explicitly quantified in two studies: Shah et al. (2021) reported periapical radiolucency in 40.4% of root canal systems, and Gambarini et al. (2018) reported periapical periodontitis in 45.1% of treated cases. Several studies documented root fracture cases or retreatment cases descriptively; however, none quantified proportional prevalence numerically. Table 3
Demographic and pathology characteristics of included study samples
Study NoAuthor(s) & YearAge Range/Mean AgeTooth Types EvaluatedBaseline Periapical Lesion RateRoot Fracture Prevalence% Retreatment Cases
1Karobari MI, et al. (2023)Not reportedMandibular premolarsNot reportedNot reportedNot reported
2Shah SJ, et al. (2021)CBCT on adult patients; specific age range not reportedMultiple tooth types40.4% root canal systems had periapical radiolucencyNot reportedNot reported
3Doğan M-S, et al. (2018)Not reportedEndodontically treated teethNot reportedReported high fracture detection; details not quantified numericallyNot specified
4Dąbrowski W, et al. (2022)Not reportedComplicated endodontic casesNot reportedNot reportedNot reported
5Kazimierczak W, et al. (2024)12–70 yearsMultiple tooth groupsNot reportedNot reportedNot reported
6Madfa AA, et al. (2023)Not reportedMaxillary 2nd molarsNot reportedNot reportedNot reported
7Yılmaz F, et al. (2016)Not reportedMultiple teethNot reportedNot reportedNot reported
8Al Yahya RS, et al. (2023)Not reportedPremolarsNot reportedNot reportedNot reported
9Gambarini G, et al. (2018)Not reportedEndodontically treated teethPeriapical periodontitis present in 45.1% of casesNRSome retreatment cases noted but not quantified
10Aristizabal-Elejalde D, et al. (2020)Not reportedEndodontically treated teeth with/without postsNot reportedSome fracture cases noted but not quantifiedNot reported
11Kadkhodayan S, et al. (2023)Not reportedMultiple tooth groupsNot reportedNot reportedNot reported
12Kalogeropoulos K, et al. (2022)Not reportedFractured instrument casesNot reportedNot reportedSome retreatment cases noted but not quantified

sec.title: Findings and treatment success
sec.p: CBCT images correlate significantly with the apex status and crown restoration and have the ability to identify structural variations and lesions that have a bearing on treatment success. A few studies emphasized that periapical lesions can be identified with more certainty with the help of CBCT, thus accurately predicting the prognosis of such lesions, which are significantly associated with treatment failure if it is not treated properly. For example, research from Shah et al. (2021) and Gambarini et al. (2018) found that CBCT is essential for the accurate visualization of periapical lesions that were occult on conventional 2D radiographs, which consequently affects treatment planning and RCT success.

Root fractures were also an important finding in CBCT. Doğan et al. (2018) and Aristizabal-Elejalde et al. (2020) demonstrated that CBCT could identify two root fractures that had been overlooked by conventional radiographs, leading to a modification of the proceeding treatment strategy. The existence of root fractures, most importantly in the case of endodontically treated teeth, was found to reduce the probability of successful treatment outcomes as the occurrence of complications after treatment was associated with fractures.

In addition, root canal configuration was evaluated in some studies, such as Al Yahya et al. (2023) in which CBCT helped in detecting intricate canal configurations which may have an impact on the effective cleaning and shaping of root canals. With appropriate identification of these configurations, the treatment results could be more favorable owing to better disinfection and obturation of the canals.

Across the included studies, the reported success rates varied between 70 and 95%, with higher success observed in cases without pre-existing periapical pathology and with adequate root filling quality. The duration of observation ranged from 6 months to 5 years, with longer follow-up studies demonstrating more reliable indicators of long-term healing and stability.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p:
Across the six diagnostic accuracy studies, the overall risk of bias was predominantly low. Al Yahya et al. (2023), Gambarini et al. (2018), Madfa et al. (2023), and Karobari et al. (2023) demonstrated low risk of bias across all QUADAS-2 domains, reflecting appropriate patient selection, well-defined CBCT index test procedures, valid reference standards, and adequate flow and timing. Doğan et al. (2018) showed a moderate risk of bias in the reference standard domain due to limited clarity regarding the verification process, although all other domains were rated low. Yılmaz et al. (2016) also exhibited moderate concerns in patient selection and reference standard due to methodological limitations, while maintaining low risk in the index test and flow/timing domains. A QUADAS-2 summary for the diagnostic accuracy studies is shown in Table 4. Table 4
QUADAS-2 Quality Assessment
Author(s) & YearPatient SelectionIndex TestReference StandardFlow/TimingOverall Risk
Al Yahya RS, et al. (2023)LowLowLowLowLow
Gambarini G, et al. (2018)LowLowLowLowLow
Doğan M-S, et al. (2018)LowLowModerateLowModerate
Madfa AA, et al. (2023)LowLowLowLowLow
Yılmaz F, et al. (2016)ModerateLowModerateLowModerate
Karobari MI, et al. (2023)LowLowLowLowLow

Most prognostic studies achieved moderate to high NOS scores, reflecting generally good methodological quality with adequate selection and outcome assessment. High risk of bias was identified in Aristizabal-Elejalde et al. (2020) and Kadkhodayan et al. (2023), primarily due to limited comparability between groups and inherent limitations of cross-sectional study designs. Table 5 shows NOS assessment for the prognostic outcome studies. Table 5
NOS Quality Assessment
Author(s) & YearSelection (4)Comparability (2)Outcome (3)Total (9)Risk
Shah SJ, et al. (2021)3126Moderate
Dąbrowski W, et al. (2022)4239Low
Kazimierczak W, et al. (2024)4138Low
Kalogeropoulos K, et al. (2022)3238Low
Aristizabal-Elejalde D, et al. (2020)2125High
Kadkhodayan S, et al. (2023)3025High

sec.title: Meta-analysis results
sec.p:
Across the six diagnostic accuracy studies, CBCT demonstrated consistently high performance in identifying key anatomical and pathological conditions relevant to endodontic prognosis. For the detection of periapical lesions, the pooled sensitivity and specificity were 0.88 and 0.84, respectively, indicating strong capability in identifying early or subtle pathology. CBCT also showed reliable diagnostic utility for vertical root fractures, with pooled estimates of 0.81 for sensitivity and 0.86 for specificity. Additionally, CBCT exhibited excellent accuracy in assessing complex root canal morphology, achieving a pooled sensitivity of 0.91 and specificity of 0.88. These results collectively support the superior diagnostic performance of CBCT over conventional radiography in detecting features that influence treatment planning and outcomes. Table 6 and Fig. 2 present the pooled diagnostic accuracy estimates derived from the included CBCT diagnostic studies. Table 6
Pooled diagnostic accuracy estimates for CBCT across included studies
OutcomeSensitivitySpecificityArea Under CurveHeterogeneity (I2)
Periapical lesions0.88 (0.82–0.92)0.84 (0.78–0.89)0.9247%
Vertical root fractures0.81 (0.72–0.88)0.86 (0.78–0.92)0.9052%
Canal morphology0.91 (0.86–0.94)0.88 (0.82–0.92)0.9428%Fig. 2
Forest plot: Meta-analysis findings for Diagnostic Studies

The prognostic meta-analysis demonstrated that specific CBCT-detected findings were significantly associated with the risk of root canal treatment failure, with periapical lesions showing an odds ratio (OR) of 2.63 (95% CI 1.78–3.58), root fractures exhibiting the strongest predictive value with an OR of 3.48 (95% CI 2.05–5.12), and complex canal anatomy also contributing to an increased likelihood of failure with an OR of 1.94 (95% CI 1.20–2.82). Table 7 and Fig. 3 summarize the pooled OR from the prognostic meta-analysis, evaluating the association between CBCT-detected variables. Table 7
Pooled Prognostic Effect of CBCT-Detected Findings on Root Canal Treatment Outcomes
PredictorPooled OR95% CIp-valueHeterogeneity (I2)
Periapical lesions2.631.78–3.58 < 0.00139%
Root fractures3.482.05–5.12 < 0.00144%
Complex canal morphology1.941.20–2.820.00428%Fig. 3
Forest plot: Meta-analysis findings for Prognostic Studies

sec.title: Subgroup analyses
sec.p:
Subgroup analyses as shown in Table 8 further indicated that studies using voxel sizes < 0.2 mm reported higher diagnostic and prognostic accuracy, and follow-up periods of ≥ 12 months yielded stronger prognostic validity. Larger periapical lesions (≥ 3 mm) were stronger predictors of endodontic failure than smaller lesions. Additionally, analyses based on tooth type indicated greater heterogeneity among molar teeth compared with anterior teeth, likely reflecting anatomical complexity and variable imaging performance in multi-rooted teeth. Table 8
Subgroup analyses examining factors influencing diagnostic and prognostic performance of CBCT
SubgroupComparisonResult
Voxel size < 0.2 mmversus ≥ 0.2 mmHigher diagnostic accuracy (p = 0.03)
Follow-up ≥ 12 monthsversus < 12 monthsStronger prognostic association (OR 2.20 vs 1.40)
Lesion size ≥ 3 mmversus < 3 mmStronger prediction of failure
Tooth typeMolars vs anterior teethHigher heterogeneity for molars

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review separately synthesized diagnostic accuracy and prognostic evidence relating to CBCT in endodontics. CBCT demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy for detecting periapical lesions, vertical root fractures, and complex canal morphology-findings consistent with established literature. Beyond diagnosis, this review also quantified how these CBCT-detected variables influence treatment prognosis. Prognostically, CBCT-identified periapical lesions, fractures, and complex anatomy were significantly associated with higher odds of RCT failure, supporting previous clinical observations that untreated complexities or unrecognized pathology adversely impact outcomes.

While the SEDENTEX guidelines and the previous landmark studies established the diagnostic value of CBCT in complex endodontic treatments, subsequent research over the past decade has expanded its role beyond diagnosis to prognostic evaluation (Patel et al. 2019; Ng et al. 2011). The present review builds upon these foundations by integrating evidence published between 2013 and 2024, quantitatively assessing how CBCT-derived parameters—such as periapical lesion detection, root fracture identification, and canal morphology—correlate with endodontic treatment success. Thus, the current analysis provides an updated evidence base that links CBCT findings with outcome prediction, thereby extending the scope of existing literature.

The findings consistently demonstrate that CBCT enhances diagnostic precision and contributes to improved treatment planning and outcomes, especially in complex endodontic scenarios. The finding that voxel sizes < 0.2 mm improved diagnostic and prognostic accuracy support previous reproducibility studies showing resolution-dependent detection of fine cortical defects and accessory canals. Differences from prior reviews likely relate to our separation of diagnostic and prognostic effects and longer follow-up periods among included prognostic cohorts. Traditional 2D radiography, while widely used, often falls short in accurately detecting periapical lesions, vertical root fractures, and complex root canal configurations due to overlapping structures and limited resolution (Patel et al. 2019; Rodríguez et al. 2017; Venkatesh and Venkatesh Elluru 2017; Alshomrani 2024). In contrast, CBCT offers high-resolution, three-dimensional imaging that enables clinicians to visualize internal and external root anatomy with greater detail, thereby improving the identification of complicating factors such as accessory canals, root fractures, and resorptive defects (Aminoshariae et al. 2018; Dalili Kajan and Taromsari 2012). In addition to traditional radiographs, other imaging techniques such as MRI and CT scans can also be used in endodontics. However, CBCT offers several advantages, such as a lower radiation dose compared to CT scans and a more focused area of imaging, making it more suitable for dental applications (Sarsam et al. 2025).

The findings of this meta-analysis demonstrate that CBCT-detected pathological factors have important prognostic implications for root canal treatment outcomes. Specifically, periapical lesions identified on CBCT were associated with a significantly increased likelihood of treatment failure (OR = 2.63), and the presence of CBCT-detected vertical root fractures showed an even stronger association with adverse outcomes (OR = 3.48). These results reinforce longstanding evidence that persistent apical pathology and undiagnosed fractures are major contributors to post-treatment disease. Furthermore, CBCT proved highly effective in detecting complex root canal configurations (sensitivity 0.91; specificity 0.88), and since untreated or missed canals are a leading cause of failure, this diagnostic advantage has clear prognostic relevance. Subgroup findings also highlighted that CBCT was particularly beneficial in cases involving complex anatomy or larger periapical lesions (≥ 3 mm), where conventional imaging often lacks sufficient sensitivity. Substantial variability existed in CBCT parameters across included studies. Only one study reported complete exposure and device information (Gambarini et al. 2018), while others provided voxel size and qualitative descriptions of FOV or protocol settings without numeric detail. Such inconsistencies reduce reproducibility and may contribute to diagnostic variability. The absence of standardized image acquisition and reliability reporting supports the need for future studies to adopt minimum reporting standards for CBCT protocols, calibration, and observer agreement statistics, particularly where diagnostic accuracy outcomes are synthesized in meta-analysis. Considerable variability existed in baseline patient and clinical characteristics. Only one study reported an age range (Kazimierczak et al. 2024) and two studies quantified periapical lesion prevalence (Shah et al. 2021; Gambarini et al. 2018). Although several studies evaluated root fractures or retreatment cases, most did not report absolute frequencies or proportions. The lack of standardized reporting of baseline pathology limits comparison across studies and may contribute to between-study heterogeneity in pooled effect estimates. Future primary studies should adopt standardized reporting for lesion status, fracture prevalence, and retreatment indicators to improve reproducibility and facilitate meta-analytic synthesis.

The overall methodological quality of the included studies ranged from low to moderate risk of bias, supporting the general reliability of the pooled estimates while emphasizing the need for future research employing standardized outcome definitions, prospective designs, and more uniform CBCT parameters.

Despite these advantages, several barriers limit the widespread use of CBCT in daily endodontic practice. These include high cost, limited availability in resource-constrained settings, and the need for specialized training to interpret CBCT images effectively (Rodríguez et al. 2017; Sarsam et al. 2025). Additionally, concerns remain regarding its radiation exposure. Although, CBCT has demonstrated significant diagnostic and prognostic advantages in endodontics, its routine use for all cases is neither justified nor advisable due to radiation exposure concerns. Consistent with the As Low As Reasonably Achievable (ALARA) principle and SEDENTEX guidelines, CBCT should be employed selectively—particularly in complex cases where conventional radiographs fail to provide sufficient diagnostic information. When indicated, a limited field of view scan should be preferred to minimize radiation dose while maintaining diagnostic accuracy (Venkatesh and Venkatesh Elluru 2017). Therefore, the current findings support the selective, evidence-based integration of CBCT into endodontic practice rather than universal application.

The potential of integrating CBCT with AI tools offers a promising solution to some of these limitations. AI-assisted interpretation can enhance diagnostic accuracy, reduce observer variability, and improve workflow efficiency (Kazimierczak et al. 2024). The use of AI in analyzing CBCT scans could particularly benefit less experienced practitioners, expanding access to expert-level diagnosis in diverse clinical settings.

To summarize, CBCT serves as a valuable adjunct in complex endodontic cases where conventional radiography is insufficient—such as in identifying missed canals, root fractures, or ambiguous periapical pathology. Its use enhances diagnostic confidence, treatment planning, and prognosis assessment. However, in accordance with ALARA and SEDENTEX guidelines, CBCT should be used selectively and justified by clear clinical need to balance diagnostic benefit with radiation safety.

sec.title: Limitations and future directions
sec.p: This review has several limitations. First, variability in CBCT parameters-such as voxel size, field of view, and exposure protocols-introduced heterogeneity that may have influenced diagnostic and prognostic estimates. Second, the number of included studies was small, preventing a reliable assessment of publication bias. Third, inconsistent definitions of treatment success and variability in reference standards across studies may have contributed to outcome variability. Fourth, exposure settings, device models, and scanner calibration were not reported in most studies, preventing assessment of radiation output or resolution equivalence across CBCT systems. These omissions introduce uncertainty in evaluating imaging-related sources of heterogeneity. Several studies did not report key demographic or pathology variables, including patient age, baseline periapical status, fracture prevalence, and retreatment proportions. These omissions limited subgroup analyses and may affect interpretation of pooled diagnostic and prognostic associations. Future multicenter studies with unified criteria, long-term follow-up, and integration of AI-based CBCT analytics are recommended.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Based on the available evidence, CBCT demonstrates enhanced ability to detect periapical lesions, root fractures, and complex canal morphology, and these findings are associated with treatment outcomes; however, the results should be interpreted with caution. The prognostic associations identified in this review do not establish causation, and CBCT findings alone cannot guarantee RCT success or failure. While CBCT offers clear advantages in specific complex clinical situations, its use should remain selective and guided by clinical necessity, operator expertise, and adherence to radiation safety principles. Routine use in all endodontic cases is not warranted, especially considering cost, accessibility, and variability in CBCT interpretation. Ultimately, CBCT should be incorporated thoughtfully as an adjunctive tool that supports but does not replace the comprehensive clinical judgment.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data set used in the current study will be made available on request from the corresponding author.

notes.title: Ethical approval
notes.p: Ethical approval was not required as the study involved analysis of published data.

notes.title: Conflict of interest
notes.p: The author declares no conflict of interest.
